It is also clear that van Gennep happened to possess an encyclopaedic knowledge with regard to the comparative study of net-making. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 2 Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries: Emile Durkheim, Marcel Mauss and Gabriel Tarde. Revisiting the Foundations of Sociology and Anthropology We must have complete liberty to think, and this includes the liberty to digress. (Arnold van Gennep, Les Demi-Savants) There arose then [after 1890] a fairly violent antagonism among the previously organized sciences of man by the renewal of Comtian sociology effected by Durkheim. He took control of the teaching of the universities, his opponents having for fiefs the Collège de France and l’Institut. In the tight group the Durkheimists mounted the assault from these positions and in approximately twenty years made themselves masters. Whoever was not part of the group was ‘marked’. (Excerpt from van Gennep’s left-behind, unpublished notes collected by Belmont, as quoted by Zumwalt) The aim of this chapter is twofold. It builds on to the framework established in Chapter 1 by reconstructing in some further detail the intellectual battle Arnold van Gennep launched against Emile Durkheim. It must be stressed from the outset that there is an intrinsic value to this exercise and for a very simple reason: this is a hugely significant chapter in our intellectual history and, quite simply, very little is known about it. Even Durkheimian sociologists largely fail to discuss or even make mention of van Gennep’s existence. In his book on Durkheim, Steven Lukes does at least remind us that Arnold van Gennep was Durkheim’s most fierce opponent: ‘The most devastating of Durkheim’s anthropological critics was the great ethnographer and folklorist Arnold van Gennep, who criticized The Elementary Forms on both empirical and theoretical grounds’ (Lukes 1985: 524). However, Lukes does not give sufficient credit or space to the substance of that critique. Having stated the core of the critique, he shies away from a real discussion of it by returning to the assumption (ibid.: 524, fn 35) that Durkheim and his followers simply ‘did not take Arnold van Gennep seriously’. 1 1 Lukes invokes this statement referring to a personal communication with M. Davy, e.g. to a conversation taking place nearly half a century after Durkheim’s death. Van Gennep had long since been catalogued as irrelevant to the social sciences; but Lukes mistakenly Liminality and the Modern 48 In a similar vein, in his discussion of The Elementary Forms of Religious Life (abbreviated as EFRL in what follows), in Fournier’s book on Marcel Mauss, mention is made of van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim, but omitting any engagement with its substance and how it actually touches the core of the entire argument. 2 One might of course argue that van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim should not take centre stage in a book on Marcel Mauss. And yet, it was Mauss who gathered most of the data and literature for Durkheim’s work, writing or co-authoring the ethnographic parts – and much more than that. Furthermore, there is little doubt that the critiques of Durkheim actually had an effect upon Mauss, and therefore influenced – in ways that are difficult to discern – his own mature sociology. It is certainly true that van Gennep was to a large extent excluded from French intellectual life. However, based on what we have seen so far and on the argument that follows it seems more plausible to suggest that this exclusion happened exactly because van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim was well-founded, precise and went straight to the heart of Durkheim’s entire academic project of creating a solid social science based on empirical facts. And this has to do with the second purpose of this chapter: it is precisely by positing van Gennep’s project against that of Durkheim that we gain a clearer picture of what it was that van Gennep was trying to establish as the foundations for the social sciences, or what he baptized as a ‘biological sociology’; and as will be briefly indicated in the final sections of this chapter, he here had quite a lot in common with the perspectives of Gabriel Tarde, another ‘forgotten classic’ of the social sciences. The concept of liminality gains further footing by marking out these differences, and also by indicating the connections to Tarde and his master concept, namely imitation. As will be discussed in the chapters that follow, imitation is indeed one of those related concepts that equips liminality with its deeper analytical value. This is because in liminal situations we are often not sure whom or what to imitate. But imitate we do. While on various occasions anthropological figures like Leach, Needham (1967) and Evans-Pritchard (1960) expressed sincere wonder as to why van Gennep had not been held in higher regard within French anthropology (‘an academic disgrace’, as Needham said; 1967: xi), they too never went deeper into the question. Even Belmont’s book on Arnold van Gennep does not really tackle the issue head-on: what actually happened; how and why exactly was van Gennep so systematically ostracized? The question has been taken up in only a very few texts, and most explicitly in an essay by Belier. Belier (1994) argued that the Durkheimians’ disregard of transfers this statement to the far from clear-cut situation of the early twentieth century in which those sciences were actually taking shape. 2 Fournier wrongly attributes van Gennep’s review of EFRL as a 1912 publication; it does not seem that Fournier has consulted the primary sources here, relying on Belmont’s reconstruction, which does not fully engage with the details of the van Gennep-Durkheim discussion. It is to be hoped that Mauss scholars will venture further into this debate.
